ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crab bee hoon or Sri Lanka crab floating noodle. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Crustaceans, cooked) is used as the closest parent anchor for this mixed crab-and-noodle dish. No composite FoodEx2 code exists for crab bee hoon specifically.

serving.description: 1 portion (shared dish, approximately 350 g per person)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Sri Lanka crab bee hoon is approximately 350 g per person, comprising crab pieces, rice vermicelli, and milky broth.

nutrient_source.title: Crab bee hoon (mixed crab and rice vermicelli dish) — estimated from component analysis using IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sri Lanka crab bee hoon exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis: cooked crab (IMR MyFCD / USDA SR Legacy), cooked rice vermicelli (IMR MyFCD), evaporated milk contribution, and broth. Values represent the composite dish per 100 g edible portion. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (bee hoon), cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; composite dish GI estimated by carbohydrate-weighted method
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sri Lanka crab bee hoon. The dominant glycemic carbohydrate source is rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of approximately 53 for cooked rice vermicelli (rice noodles, white, cooked). Crab and broth contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Composite dish GI is estimated at 53 (low). Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as: (53 × 10.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5) / 100 = approximately 19.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~1120 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt broth preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 1120 mg sodium, representing nearly half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli (~19 GL per serving) despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that while the GI of rice vermicelli is low (GI ~53), a full restaurant portion delivers a moderate glycemic load (~19 GL); portion control and pairing with additional protein from crab can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium and cholesterol content; low saturated fat relative to total energy
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to sodium (~1120 mg per serving) and cholesterol (~193 mg per serving from crab); the dish is relatively low in saturated fat (~1.1 g per 100 g) and provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from crab, which may partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sri Lanka Crab Bee Hoon (Floating Noodle) is a premium Singaporean-Malaysian seafood noodle dish featuring live Sri Lanka crab (Portunus pelagicus or Scylla serrata) cooked in a rich, milky broth made from evaporated milk, chicken stock, garlic, ginger, and white pepper, served over rice vermicelli (bee hoon) that floats in the creamy soup. The dish is a signature offering at zi char (Chinese-style cook-to-order) restaurants in Singapore, particularly at establishments such as Marina Seafood House at Boat Quay. The broth is characteristically white and mildly sweet-savoury, with the crab imparting natural sweetness and umami. The rice vermicelli absorbs the flavourful broth, creating a soft, silky texture. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density (~98 kcal per 100 g), high-quality protein from crab (~8.5 g per 100 g), and is relatively low in saturated fat. Sodium content is notable (~320 mg per 100 g) due to the seasoned broth. The glycemic index is low (~53) driven by rice vermicelli, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~19 GL). Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to sodium and glycemic load considerations. The crab contributes cholesterol (~55 mg per 100 g) and beneficial micronutrients including zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sri Lanka crab bee hoon exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR MyFCD, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using IMR MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy data for cooked crab and cooked rice vermicelli. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice vermicelli. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no single approved source entry exists for this composite dish. Values represent the edible portion of the composite dish per 100 g including broth. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR MyFCD crab entry used. 4. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in cooked crab and rice vermicelli per USDA SR Legacy. 9. GI set to 53 based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice vermicelli (analogous estimate); gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 10. The dish is not certified halal as it is served at a zi char restaurant where pork-based ingredients may be used in the kitchen. 11. Food group reclassified from 'Grains and staples' (input) to 'Mixed' as the dish is a composite of seafood and noodles. 12. Serving size of 350 g per person is an estimate for a shared restaurant portion.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built. food_group reclassified from 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' to reflect composite seafood-noodle nature. All mandatory multilingual names added for Singaporean cuisine (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). GI set to 53 from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for cooked rice vermicelli. All -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Aliases consistency check performed and verified.

Iteration 2: Verified GI category threshold: GI 53 ≤ 55 → 'low' confirmed correct. Verified all aliases_by_language strings present verbatim in flat aliases. Verified no zero macros without justification. Verified ontology match_type values are only 'exact', 'closest_parent', or 'none'. All hard gates confirmed passing. Score 8/8 confirmed.
